data.title: Does Dollar General’s 40% Rally Signal More Upside After New Store Growth Announcement?
data.description:
If you’re watching Dollar General and wondering if now is the time to buy, hold, or steer clear, you’re not alone. There’s plenty to consider in the current market, and few stocks have bounced around as much as this one. After enduring some rocky years, Dollar General’s shares have powered ahead by 40.6% so far this year, including a robust 8.3% gain in the past week alone. The sentiment shift is hard to ignore, especially with a 1-year return of 32.6%. On the other hand, the 5-year and 3-year returns remain deeply negative, suggesting that optimism is breaking through what has been a lengthy period of doubt.

Much of this turnaround is linked to evolving consumer trends and a market that is warming up to retailers serving value-conscious shoppers. As economic uncertainty persists, investors are reassessing the risks and growth potential in discount retail. Dollar General appears to be reaping some of those rewards in its recent price movements, as more people hunt for low prices in their everyday shopping.

But is the stock truly a good value right now? Using widely accepted methods, Dollar General scores 4 out of a possible 6 on our valuation scorecard, which means it is considered undervalued in most key metrics. In the next section, I’ll break down what is driving that score, and how the most common approaches to valuation compare. Stay with me until the end, because I’ll also share a more powerful way to judge a stock’s real worth.

Dollar General delivered 32.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Consumer Retailing industry.

Approach 1: Dollar General Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model aims to estimate a company’s intrinsic value by calculating the present value of expected future cash flows. For Dollar General, this means taking projections of its Free Cash Flow (FCF) for each year and discounting those values back to today to get a total estimate of what the company is really worth per share.

According to the model, Dollar General generated $1.56 billion in FCF over the last twelve months. Analyst estimates forecast steady growth, with FCF projected to rise to $1.83 billion by 2030. Beyond 2029, projections are based on reasonable growth rates applied by the analysis platform. This model uses a “2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity” approach, incorporating both direct analyst estimates for the next five years and then extrapolating out further based on industry trends.

The DCF model calculates Dollar General's intrinsic value at $157.05 per share. This is around 32.3% higher than the current market price, implying the stock is substantially undervalued by this standard. This margin of safety suggests there could be upside potential through the lens of expected cash generation and risk-adjusted discounting.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Dollar General Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Dollar General, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely used valuation metric because it highlights how much investors are paying for each dollar of company earnings. A lower PE may signal better value, while a higher PE could suggest growth expectations or premium positioning in the market.

It is important to remember that a "normal" or "fair" PE ratio is shaped by expectations for a company's future growth and its risk profile. Companies with higher growth prospects or a more stable earnings outlook usually command higher PE ratios. Those facing more uncertainty or slower growth often trade at lower multiples.

Currently, Dollar General trades at a PE ratio of 19.6x. This sits slightly above the average for its direct peers at 19.0x, but below the broader Consumer Retailing industry average of 21.4x. These comparisons provide helpful context but can overlook important company-specific factors.

This is where Simply Wall St’s "Fair Ratio" comes in. The Fair Ratio for Dollar General, calculated at 22.5x, takes into account not only its growth potential and risks but also its profit margin, size, and the industry backdrop. Unlike simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio reflects a more complete picture of what investors should be willing to pay based on Dollar General’s actual qualities and market context.

When comparing Dollar General’s current PE of 19.6x with its Fair Ratio of 22.5x, the stock appears to be undervalued on this basis. This may indicate a potential value opportunity for investors who believe in its growth runway and risk profile.

data.title: Did Colliers International Group's (TSX:CIGI) Acquisitions and Rebranding Redefine Its Long-Term Investment Strategy?
data.description:
• Colliers International Group recently presented at the Ottawa Real Estate Forum, where leadership highlighted strong Q2 2025 results, a rebranding of its Investment Management division as Harrison Street Asset Management, and the acquisition of RoundShield Partners to expand in credit, student housing, and hospitality.
  • The merger of Rockwood Capital with Harrison Street under Colliers' umbrella marks a significant move to broaden investment strategy capabilities and add leadership depth as part of the company's ongoing growth initiatives.
  • We'll explore how Colliers' expanded capabilities in credit and student housing, following recent acquisitions, affect its long-term investment narrative.

Colliers International Group Investment Narrative Recap

Being a Colliers International shareholder often comes down to believing in the company's ability to diversify its service offerings and earnings streams through consistent expansion and acquisitions. The recent announcement of stronger Q2 2025 revenue and expanded capabilities via the Harrison Street and RoundShield developments supports Colliers’ efforts to grow recurring fee income streams, though execution risk from integrating these acquisitions remains the main short-term catalyst as well as the greatest risk to watch; for now, the news seems to reinforce, but not fundamentally alter, these drivers.

Among the developments highlighted, the merger of Rockwood Capital into Harrison Street under Colliers’ umbrella stands out for its potential to expand investment management capabilities, particularly in real assets and alternative strategies. By leveraging Rockwood’s expertise alongside Harrison Street, Colliers seeks to boost fee-based earnings, a current catalyst for the business, even as it faces broader macroeconomic and fundraising headwinds.
